claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05); Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK

Child is 16 and in approved education
Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please tell me each of their ages (or names and ages) and we can assess them together.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and want to check if I'm eligible.

I have two children actually - Alex, who's 6 years old, and Blake, who's 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! You're very welcome - I'm happy to help you check your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Since you have two children in different age categories, let me assess them both:

- **Alex (6 years old)** - Under 16, so automatically qualifies based on age
- **Blake (16 years old)** - In the 16-19 category, so I'll need to check a few more details

Let me navigate through the assessment for each child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! Now I have information about both paths. Let me ask you some questions for each child:

---

**For Alex (age 6):**

Since Alex is under 16, there's just one quick check:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

*(At age 6, I'd expect the answer is yes - not working - but I need to confirm)*

---

**For Blake (age 16):**

I need to understand Blake's current situation. Which of the following best describes Blake?

1. **In education or training** - Currently enrolled in approved education or training (such as school, college, or an approved training programme)
2. **Recently left education** - Has left education within the last few months
3. **Neither** - Not in education/training and hasn't been recently

---

Please let me know the answers for both children and we'll continue the assessment!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh, of course! 

For Alex - no, he's only 6, so he's not working at all.

For Blake - yes, he's in education. He's studying for his A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that!

**For Alex** - Great, that's what I expected. Let me continue his assessment.

**For Blake** - Studying A-levels at college is exactly the kind of approved education that can qualify. Let me check the specific requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! A-levels are on the approved qualifications list. Let me verify a few more details for Blake:

**For Blake (A-levels at college):**

A-levels are approved education for Child Benefit purposes. I just need to confirm a couple of things:

1. **Is Blake studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study/course time)

2. **Is Blake's education funded by an employer, or does Blake have an agreement with an employer for a future job?** (This would typically be "no" for standard A-level students at college)

3. **Is Blake working alongside their studies?** If yes, is it **less than 24 hours per week**?

---

**For Alex** - I'll continue his assessment in the background once we've sorted Blake's details.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Blake:

1. Yes, he's studying full-time at college.

2. No, it's just normal college A-levels - nothing to do with an employer.

3. No, Blake doesn't work - he just focuses on his studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great news! Blake's situation is exactly what qualifies for Child Benefit - full-time A-levels with no employer involvement and not working.

Let me continue the assessment for both children now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Now I need to check a few more things that apply to both Alex and Blake:

**Are either of your children receiving benefits in their own name?**

Specifically, are Alex or Blake receiving:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA), or
- Universal Credit

in their own right (not as part of your household claim)?

*(This would be unusual, especially for a 6-year-old, but I need to confirm for both children)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receives any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming. Let me continue the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Quick question about apprenticeships:

**Is either Alex or Blake in an apprenticeship?**

- For Alex (age 6), I assume the answer is no
- For Blake, you mentioned he's doing A-levels at college - just confirming he's not also doing any kind of apprenticeship programme?

*(If neither child is in an apprenticeship, we can move on to the next stage of the assessment)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them is in an apprenticeship. Blake is just doing his A-levels at college, and Alex is only 6, so definitely not!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Ha! Yes, definitely not for Alex! 😊

Thank you for confirming. Now we've established that both children qualify as "eligible children" for Child Benefit purposes. The next step is to check your situation as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You'll qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and meet one of these conditions:
   - Working and earning above the National Insurance threshold
   - Actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
   - You have sufficient resources to support yourself and aren't claiming certain benefits
   - You're a student with sufficient resources
   - You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with the right to reside
4. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad (e.g., diplomat, armed forces)

Which of these applies to your situation? For most people, option 1 (normally living in the UK) is the answer.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, I don't live in the UK. I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. That's an important detail!

Living abroad doesn't automatically disqualify you, but it does narrow down the options. Let me ask a follow-up question:

**Are you a Crown servant living abroad?**

This would include:
- Working for the UK government overseas (e.g., diplomat, embassy staff)
- Serving in the UK armed forces stationed abroad
- Certain other government roles in qualifying countries

If not, could you tell me a bit more about your situation? For example:
- What country are you living in?
- What is your connection to the UK? (e.g., UK citizen, EU citizen with settled/pre-settled status)
- Why are you living abroad? (work, family, etc.)

This will help me determine if you might still qualify for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or anything like that. I'm just living abroad - nothing to do with government work or the military.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Unfortunately, this is significant for your eligibility.

Based on what you've told me, you don't meet any of the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit:

- ❌ You don't normally live in the UK
- ❌ You're not a Crown servant living abroad
- ❌ The EU Settlement Scheme paths (settled/pre-settled status) are for people living in the UK

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry to share this news, but based on your current situation:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Assessment Result: NOT ELIGIBLE

I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit** for either Alex or Blake.

### Summary:

| Child | Qualifying Child? | Eligible for Benefit? |
|-------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Alex** (age 6) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Blake** (age 16) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

### Reason:
Both of your children meet the criteria to be "qualifying children" for Child Benefit purposes. However, **you do not meet the UK residency requirements** because you are living abroad and are not in a qualifying category (such as being a Crown servant).

### What you can do:

1. **If you return to the UK** - You would likely become eligible once you're normally resident in the UK again. Both children would qualify at that point.

2. **If someone else in the UK cares for the children** - If there's another person (such as a partner or family member) who lives in the UK and is responsible for Alex and Blake, they may be able to claim Child Benefit instead.

3. **Check with HMRC directly** - If you think your circumstances might be more complex (e.g., you have ties to the UK, or your situation may change soon), you can contact HMRC for personalised advice:
   - **Child Benefit helpline**: 0300 200 3100
   - **Website**: [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit)

I'm sorry I couldn't bring better news. Is there anything else you'd like to know or any other questions I can help with?

reasonings: Alex (age 6) qualifies as an eligible child - he is under 16 and not working. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad and are not a Crown servant or in any other qualifying category.

Blake (age 16) qualifies as an eligible child - he is in approved full-time education (A-levels), not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad and are not a Crown servant or in any other qualifying category.
arguments.overall_reasoning: While both children (Alex, age 6, and Blake, age 16 studying A-levels) meet the criteria to be qualifying children for Child Benefit, the claimant is not eligible because they do not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad and is not a Crown servant or in any other special qualifying category that would allow them to claim Child Benefit from outside the UK.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 6) qualifies as an eligible child - he is under 16 and not working. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad and are not a Crown servant or in any other qualifying category.

Blake (age 16) qualifies as an eligible child - he is in approved full-time education (A-levels), not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad and are not a Crown servant or in any other qualifying category.

response.overall_reasoning: While both children (Alex, age 6, and Blake, age 16 studying A-levels) meet the criteria to be qualifying children for Child Benefit, the claimant is not eligible because they do not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad and is not a Crown servant or in any other special qualifying category that would allow them to claim Child Benefit from outside the UK.
